---
title: Statistical Foundations
description: "The conceptual bedrock of applied statistics: measurement, populations, sampling, estimation theory, and likelihood"
---

Before any specific test or model, an analyst needs a firm grasp of the vocabulary and logic that underlies every statistical procedure. This area walks through what it means to measure a phenomenon, how a sample relates to the population it is drawn from, why the central limit theorem is the workhorse of inference, and how likelihood turns data into a scoring rule for competing hypotheses.

Each tutorial is self-contained and written so that a reader can land on any one page and understand the topic without prior context. Code examples use R throughout, with simulated data whenever real datasets would obscure the concept under discussion.

## What you will learn

- Scales of measurement (nominal, ordinal, interval, ratio) and how they constrain the statistics you can compute
- The distinction between a population parameter and a sample estimate, and why this matters for interpretation
- How sampling distributions arise and why they are more fundamental than the distribution of the raw data
- The central limit theorem and its practical implications for inference on means
- Point estimation, unbiasedness, consistency, efficiency, and the standard error
- Maximum likelihood estimation as a general principle for fitting models
- Sufficient statistics and information in a sample (Fisher information)

## Suggested order

Readers new to statistics may want to proceed in the order given in the sidebar; readers reviewing for a specific concept can jump directly to the relevant tutorial.

<!-- card-grid-injected -->

## Tutorials

::: {.card-grid}

::: {.card}
::: {.kicker}
TUTORIAL
:::
### [Bias and Variance of Estimators](bias-and-variance.qmd)
Decomposing an estimator's mean squared error into systematic bias and sampling variance
:::

::: {.card}
::: {.kicker}
TUTORIAL
:::
### [Big-Op and Little-op Notation](big-op-little-op.qmd)
Stochastic order symbols for describing rates of convergence and stochastic magnitudes
:::

::: {.card}
::: {.kicker}
TUTORIAL
:::
### [Characteristic Functions](characteristic-functions.qmd)
Fourier transforms of distributions: always exist, uniquely identify the distribution, and drive convergence theorems
:::

::: {.card}
::: {.kicker}
TUTORIAL
:::
### [Confidence Intervals: Introduction](confidence-intervals-introduction.qmd)
Interval estimates with a coverage guarantee, and how they differ from credible intervals
:::

::: {.card}
::: {.kicker}
TUTORIAL
:::
### [Convergence in Distribution](convergence-in-distribution.qmd)
The weakest standard mode of convergence, underlying the central limit theorem
:::

::: {.card}
::: {.kicker}
TUTORIAL
:::
### [Convergence in Probability](convergence-in-probability.qmd)
The mode of convergence that underpins consistency of estimators
:::

::: {.card}
::: {.kicker}
TUTORIAL
:::
### [Estimators and Estimation](estimators-and-estimation.qmd)
Point estimation and the plug-in principle: turning a sample into a guess about a population parameter
:::

::: {.card}
::: {.kicker}
TUTORIAL
:::
### [Fisher Information](fisher-information.qmd)
How much the data tell you about a parameter, measured by the curvature of the log-likelihood
:::

::: {.card}
::: {.kicker}
TUTORIAL
:::
### [Jensen's Inequality](jensens-inequality.qmd)
The expectation of a convex function is at least the convex function of the expectation
:::

::: {.card}
::: {.kicker}
TUTORIAL
:::
### [Likelihood Ratio Tests](likelihood-ratio-tests.qmd)
Comparing nested models by the ratio of maximised likelihoods, with Wilks' asymptotic chi-squared
:::

::: {.card}
::: {.kicker}
TUTORIAL
:::
### [Markov and Chebyshev Inequalities](chebyshev-markov.qmd)
Tail bounds derived from moments, with the LLN as a simple consequence
:::

::: {.card}
::: {.kicker}
TUTORIAL
:::
### [Maximum Likelihood Estimation](maximum-likelihood-estimation.qmd)
The likelihood function, score equations, and asymptotic properties of the MLE
:::

::: {.card}
::: {.kicker}
TUTORIAL
:::
### [Moments and Moment Generating Functions](moments-and-mgfs.qmd)
Moments summarise distributions; the MGF is a generating function that often characterises them uniquely
:::

::: {.card}
::: {.kicker}
TUTORIAL
:::
### [Order Statistics](order-statistics.qmd)
Distributions of the sorted sample and their role in quantile theory and robust estimation
:::

::: {.card}
::: {.kicker}
TUTORIAL
:::
### [Pivotal Quantities](pivotal-quantities.qmd)
Functions of data and parameter whose distribution does not depend on the parameter, and the CIs they produce
:::

::: {.card}
::: {.kicker}
TUTORIAL
:::
### [Population vs. Sample](population-vs-sample.qmd)
Target population, sampling frame, and the distinction between parameters and statistics
:::

::: {.card}
::: {.kicker}
TUTORIAL
:::
### [Sampling Distributions](sampling-distributions.qmd)
The distribution of a statistic across repeated samples: the key object that makes inference possible
:::

::: {.card}
::: {.kicker}
TUTORIAL
:::
### [Sampling Methods](sampling-methods.qmd)
Simple random, stratified, cluster, systematic, and convenience sampling -- and when each is appropriate
:::

::: {.card}
::: {.kicker}
TUTORIAL
:::
### [Scales of Measurement](scales-of-measurement.qmd)
Nominal, ordinal, interval, and ratio scales, and which statistics each legitimately supports
:::

::: {.card}
::: {.kicker}
TUTORIAL
:::
### [Slutsky's Theorem](slutsky-theorem.qmd)
Combining convergence in distribution and in probability for asymptotic arguments
:::

::: {.card}
::: {.kicker}
TUTORIAL
:::
### [Sufficient Statistics](sufficient-statistics.qmd)
Summaries that capture every bit of information about a parameter, with the factorisation theorem and exponential family
:::

::: {.card}
::: {.kicker}
TUTORIAL
:::
### [The Cauchy-Schwarz Inequality](cauchy-schwarz.qmd)
The inner-product inequality that bounds correlations and drives many variance inequalities
:::

::: {.card}
::: {.kicker}
TUTORIAL
:::
### [The Central Limit Theorem](central-limit-theorem.qmd)
Why the sampling distribution of the mean is approximately normal, and what that means for everyday inference
:::

::: {.card}
::: {.kicker}
TUTORIAL
:::
### [The Cramer-Rao Lower Bound](cramer-rao-lower-bound.qmd)
The smallest variance an unbiased estimator can achieve, derived from Fisher information
:::

::: {.card}
::: {.kicker}
TUTORIAL
:::
### [The Delta Method](delta-method.qmd)
Asymptotic distributions of smooth functions of estimators, via a first-order Taylor expansion
:::

::: {.card}
::: {.kicker}
TUTORIAL
:::
### [The Empirical Distribution Function](empirical-distribution-function.qmd)
The sample-based step-function estimator of the CDF, with the DKW inequality for its uniform error
:::

::: {.card}
::: {.kicker}
TUTORIAL
:::
### [The Glivenko-Cantelli Theorem](glivenko-cantelli.qmd)
The uniform LLN for CDFs: the ECDF converges almost surely to the true CDF at every point simultaneously
:::

::: {.card}
::: {.kicker}
TUTORIAL
:::
### [The Law of Large Numbers](law-of-large-numbers.qmd)
Why the sample mean converges to the population mean as the sample grows, in weak and strong forms
:::

::: {.card}
::: {.kicker}
TUTORIAL
:::
### [The Method of Moments](method-of-moments.qmd)
Estimate parameters by equating theoretical moments to their sample counterparts
:::

::: {.card}
::: {.kicker}
TUTORIAL
:::
### [The Standard Error](standard-error.qmd)
The standard deviation of a statistic's sampling distribution, and how to compute it in R
:::

::: {.card}
::: {.kicker}
TUTORIAL
:::
### [Unbiasedness, Consistency, Efficiency](unbiasedness-consistency-efficiency.qmd)
Three core finite- and large-sample properties that let us compare estimators
:::

:::
